sit: The other team is trying to enter your zone 4-on-4. Your team has set up in the neutral zone with all four players between the puck and your net. The puck carrier is coming at you. What's the key to making this work?
opts: Everyone chase the puck carrier at the same time to surround them and force a quick turnover
Hold your positions and make the puck carrier make a decision — force them one way and take away the easiest options
All four players skate backwards to your own blue line to protect the net area immediately
One player steps up to challenge while the other three retreat all the way back to the crease

sit: You have the puck in your own zone under pressure. You have two options: a tricky cross-ice pass to a teammate at the blue line, or a simple short pass to your D partner three feet away. Which do you take?
opts: The tricky cross-ice pass — it leads to a faster breakout
The simple short pass to your D partner — take the safe option under pressure and let them move the puck from a better position
Try to carry it yourself around the back
Wait for a better option to appear because one of your teammates will probably get open soon if you just hold the puck

sit: Your team is defending in your own zone. The puck is in the far corner. The other team has three attackers in the zone and you have two defenders and one forward back. Where should the forward position?
opts: Skate down into the corner to help your partner battle for the puck and win it back quickly
Hold the high slot — be between the corner player and the open attackers in the high-danger area
Go all the way to your blue line
Follow the nearest attacker wherever they go around the zone and stay glued to them tightly

sit: The other team takes a shot and your goalie makes the save. The rebound goes to the corner. An opposing player is racing to it. What do you do?
opts: Watch carefully to see if your goalie covers the rebound or plays the puck before you commit to going into the corner
Get to the rebound before the opposing player — or at least get your body position between them and the puck
Call loudly for your goalie to come out and play the puck in the corner before the opposing player can get to it first
Back up all the way to the crease

sit: Your D picks up the puck in your own corner. They're looking up but they can't see behind them. Your winger is open up the wall but a forechecker is about to close on them from the blind side. What do you do?
opts: Say nothing and let the D figure it out themselves
Call out 'man on!' or tell the winger to hold — give the information that only you can see
Skate over yourself to get between the forechecker and the winger before the pass arrives
Call for the pass yourself

sit: You're defending in your own zone. The puck is in the corner behind your net. Your assignment is an attacker near the half-wall on the opposite side. The puck is far away from them. Do you watch the puck or watch your man?
opts: Watch the puck carefully because it's way more important to know where the puck is than to know where your assigned man is
Watch your man — keep them in front of you so you know when they move. You can track the puck with your peripheral vision.
Watch both equally at once
Go get the puck yourself in the corner

sit: You're playing hard in the offensive zone on a long shift. The puck turns over and the other team is breaking out. Your legs are tired. What's the responsible play?
opts: Push through your tiredness because the play needs you and your linemates are counting on you to come back on the backcheck
If you can't backcheck at full speed, get off and get a fresh player on. A tired player is worse than no player.
Slow jog back to the D-zone
Call out to the other team to stop their rush and give your team a chance to get set up in the defensive zone properly

sit: You have the puck at center ice. Three options: your winger is open on the left but a defender is closing on them. Your center is open in the slot with a clear lane. Your D is open at the blue line on the right. What do you do?
opts: Pass to the winger on the left — they were the first option you saw in your vision
Pass to the center in the slot — open lane, dangerous position, immediate scoring threat
Pass to the D on the right — they're the safest option and nobody is close to them
Carry it in yourself

sit: A forward is coming at you on a breakaway. You're standing on your goal line. What should you do with your position?
opts: Stay flat on your goal line
Skate out toward the shooter to cut the angle — give them less net to shoot at
Skate all the way out past your blue line to challenge the shooter far from the net
Lie flat down on the ice right away and hope the shooter misses the net entirely

sit: Two attackers are coming at your lone defenseman on a 2-on-1. The puck carrier has the puck. What's the goalie's job?
opts: Skate out of the crease to challenge the puck carrier and cut down their shooting angle before they can fire a shot at you
Stay in your crease, be ready for a shot, and watch for the pass — the D takes away the pass, you take the shot
Yell at your defenseman to do something
Come out past the crease to the top

sit: A shot is coming low along the ice. What's the most effective way to stop it?
opts: Try to kick the puck away with one foot while standing up on your skates the whole time
Drop into butterfly position — both knees down, pads flared out to cover the bottom
Fall sideways quickly
Stand straight up and hope that the shot hits you somewhere on your leg pads or chest

sit: The puck comes behind your net. Your winger is coming back to get it but they're a second away. You can get there first. Should you play it?
opts: Never touch the puck because it's too risky and leaving the crease always gives the other team a chance to score on the empty net
Yes — if you can get there first and your winger is a step away, stop it for them so they have a clean pickup
Only if the other team definitely isn't coming for the puck too and you have a completely clear path behind your net to play it
Kick it with your skate to the corner

sit: The puck is in the corner to your left. An attacker has it but hasn't shot. Where should you be in your net?
opts: In the middle of the net
Touching the left post — take away the near side
All the way across touching the far right post
Out of the crease to cut the angle

sit: The puck is bouncing in the crease area and both you and your defenseman are going for it. What should you say?
opts: Say nothing and grab it
Yell 'Mine!' loud and clear so your defenseman knows to get out of the way
Tap them on the shoulder pad
Wait until after the play is over to talk about who should have gone for the puck

sit: Three players from both teams are battling in front of your net. Sticks and bodies everywhere. The puck is somewhere in the mess. What's your priority?
opts: Watch the bodies carefully
Keep your eyes moving — track the puck through the traffic. Move with the puck, not with the players.
Wait patiently for the puck to come out of the pile into a clear area where you can see it and make a save
Drop immediately into your butterfly to cover the bottom of the net in case the puck squeaks out right at the goal line

sit: The puck is on the half-wall. You're set on the post. A player cuts to the back door completely open on the far side. What do you do?
opts: Stay on your near post
Quickly push across to take away the back-door pass — your near post is less dangerous
Skate out hard toward the puck carrier to challenge them right at the top of the crease
Stay exactly where you are

sit: You make a save and the puck is loose right in front of you. Two opposing players are about to get to it before your team. Should you cover it or try to play it?
opts: Always cover the puck for a faceoff
Read the situation — if two opponents are about to get it, cover it immediately. If your team has it, leave it for them.
Always leave the puck for your team no matter what's happening in front of you because your team needs to start the breakout play
Kick the puck away from the crease as hard as you can to anywhere so the other team can't get a second shot on you at all

sit: A shooter is coming at you. You notice their shoulder is turned slightly toward the left side of the net before they shoot. What does this tell you?
opts: Nothing — ignore the shoulder and just play the puck straight up the way you would with any other shooter coming at you
They're likely shooting to your right — their body is opening to the left which usually means the shot goes
They're going to deke around you to the left
They'll pass

sit: You're down and out of position — the shooter has a completely open net. You can't get back to your feet in time. What do you do?
opts: Give up the goal and just see what happens next because there's really nothing else you can do from flat on the ice like this
Use whatever you can reach — your stick, your glove, your skate — to get something in the way. Any part of you in the lane is better than nothing.
Stay completely still on the ice and hope the shooter somehow misses the wide-open net that you've given them with the mistake
Call for help from your defensemen so they can slide in front of the shooter and take away the shooting lane from where you're down

sit: The puck moves quickly from the left corner to the right corner. You were on your left post. What's the fastest way to get to the right post?
opts: Skate slowly and carefully across the crease to make sure you don't lose your balance or get caught out of position by the play
Push off the left post and use a strong T-push or shuffle across — keep your eyes on the puck the whole time
Turn around and skate quickly across
Stay on the left post a bit too long and hope the puck comes back to your side before you commit to moving across the crease

sit: One of your teammates stays at center ice while the rest of the team is defending in your own zone. They're waiting for a long pass. Your team is defending 4 against 5. What's the mistake?
opts: They're being smart by waiting for the breakaway so they can score the moment the puck gets flipped up to them from the D-zone
They're cherry picking — not helping on defense. Everyone needs to defend when the puck is in your zone. Hanging back leaves your team short-handed.
The coach told them to stay there in case the puck came up the ice, so they're just following the game plan they were given
They're too tired to skate all the way back and help in the defensive zone, so they're just conserving their energy for later

sit: Your teammate has the puck behind the net. A forechecker is coming hard from the left. Your teammate can't see them. Nobody on your team says anything. Your teammate gets hit and loses the puck. What's the mistake?
opts: The teammate should have eyes in the back of their head to see the forechecker coming behind them even without turning to look around
Nobody communicated — someone should have yelled 'man on left!' to warn their teammate about the pressure coming. Communication prevents turnovers.
The forechecker was too fast to stop
The teammate should have passed the puck much sooner so they wouldn't have gotten caught behind the net with pressure coming in

sit: An opposing forward gets past your defensemen and has a clear path to your net. You're the closest forward and you're behind them but skating hard. You can't catch them. What happens next?
opts: You give up on the play because the forward is gone and catching them from behind is pretty much impossible at this point anyway
You keep skating hard and try to get your stick in the shooting lane — even a stick in the lane can disrupt the shot or force a wider angle
You yell loudly at your defensemen for letting the attacker get behind them so they know not to make the same mistake again
You coast back slowly toward your own net because there's no chance of catching up and you want to save some energy for later

sit: You have the puck at the point. Two of your teammates are standing in front of the goalie, blocking their view. The goalie can't see you. What happens next?
opts: You pass to the corner to a teammate who can then cycle the puck down low and set up a much better scoring chance from below the goal line
You shoot through the screen — the goalie can't see the puck, so even a low shot through traffic has a great chance of going in or creating a rebound
You skate in closer to the net so you get a better angle, and then you shoot once you have a cleaner look past the screen players
You wait patiently for a better passing lane to open up so the screening teammates have a chance to reposition themselves in the slot

sit: Your D has the puck behind the net. Two forecheckers are coming from both sides. Your center is open in the middle of the ice, but the middle is risky. Your winger is on the wall. What happens next?
opts: The D tries to carry it out alone up the middle by skating through both forecheckers with quick stickhandling moves
D makes the safe play and passes to the winger on the wall — the middle is too risky with two forecheckers closing, and the boards are the safe route out
The D shoots it hard down the entire length of the ice to get the puck safely out and reset everything from a neutral position
The D freezes the puck against the boards behind the net to force a faceoff and get a quick shift change for the tired line

sit: Your team dumps the puck into the offensive zone. Your line has been on the ice for over a minute and everyone is tired. The other team is retrieving the puck behind their net. What happens next?
opts: You forecheck hard even though you're tired because backing off now means the other team gets a clean, easy breakout up the ice
Your line changes — the puck is deep in their zone and they're behind their net, so this is the perfect time for a line change. Fresh legs coming on.
You stand still at the blue line and rest a few seconds to catch your breath before chasing the puck back down into the zone
You call timeout so your tired line can get a proper rest on the bench before the other team starts moving the puck up the ice

sit: Your team has a 3-on-2. The puck carrier drives wide and draws one defender. The second defender is cheating toward the puck carrier too, leaving the far-side forward wide open. What happens next?
opts: Puck carrier keeps going hard up the ice alone and tries to beat both defenders with pure speed down the outside lane
Puck carrier sees the second defender cheat and makes the cross-ice pass to the wide-open forward for a high-quality scoring chance
Puck carrier dumps it into the zone so the forwards can chase and create a new play with an aggressive forecheck
Puck carrier stops and waits for the trailing forward to catch up before making any move into the offensive zone at all
